revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'wanted by Interpol' changed to 'subject of Interpol-channel arrest request from Bangladesh' — no public Red Notice confirmed; Home Ministry said it received a request letter, not a red notice
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Every month of delay costs workers RM25,000 each' was INCORRECT — RM25,000 is total recruitment cost/debt burden, not monthly. Removed.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Cabinet approval was 14 Aug 2024, contract finalised 3 Sep 2024 — not 'same month' as MACC NFA (Sep 2024). Timeline tightened but not simultaneous.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'No coalition will dismantle it because everyone benefits' softened to evidence-supported claim about structural persistence across administrations
CORRECTED (Stage 3): '10-agency cartel' — 'cartel' is characterization by critics, not judicial finding. Changed to '10-agency restriction'
CORRECTED (Stage 3): MACC chief Azam Baki probe is a separate 2026 matter from the Sep 2024 NFA on Bestinet — removed conflation
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Bangladesh 'agreed to stop investigating' is unverified — Bernama still reported extradition as active matter in Oct 2025. Changed to 'investigation status unclear'
CORRECTED (Stage 3): RM215 fee includes RM130 recovery costs (2018-2024) and RM85 operating costs (2025-2031) per PAC/Bernama

ADDED (Stage 2): Gender dimension — women migrant domestic workers excluded from Employment Act protections, face distinct vulnerabilities; nearly 29% of migrant domestic workers met ILO forced-labour indicators
ADDED (Stage 2): Geographic context — FWCMS does not apply in Sabah/Sarawak which have immigration autonomy under MA63; Sabah has ~1 million undocumented migrants with separate systems
ADDED (Stage 4-GPT): 24 unauthorized FWCMS users approved employer applications — PAC finding shows internal state control failure, not just vendor monopoly
ADDED (Stage 4-GPT): Employment Act requires employers to pay permit fees, not workers — the RM215 is legally employer-borne but illegally passed to workers
ADDED (Stage 4-DeepSeek): Bestinet reduced visa processing from 120 days to 14 days — acknowledging system delivers speed/scale value
ADDED (Stage 4-DeepSeek): Government attempted open tender in 2023 — only Bestinet and MyEG bid; MyEG disqualified. Alternatives are limited.
ADDED (Stage 4-DeepSeek): Contract requires Bestinet to hand over FWCMS and ownership to government at no cost before 2031 expiry
ADDED (Stage 4-GPT): International parallel — Philippines POEA operates government-owned processing; South Korea EPS reduces brokerage rents; Singapore enforces employer-pays principle
ADDED (Stage 4-GPT): SUHAKAM, C4, ILO have articulated structural reform agendas already — victim-centred protection, procurement reform, recruitment transparency

cards.text: Malaysia's migrant worker visa system is run by a single company whose founder faces an Interpol-channel arrest request from Bangladesh — and the government just extended his contract through 2031.
cards.sub: Bloomberg's 10-month investigation found Bestinet founder Aminul Islam's companies generated over RM400 million in profit in a decade. Workers pay up to RM25,000 in total recruitment costs — more than double what Bangladeshis paid before Bestinet. MACC closed its probe with 'no further action' in September 2024. Cabinet had approved the contract extension the month before.

cards.h: The government ran the system for six years without a signed contract — then set the per-worker fee at RM215, more than double its own approved rate
cards.s: PAC chastised the government in July 2024 for operating FWCMS since 2018 with only a Letter of Acceptance. PAC also found 24 unauthorized users had approved employer applications — an internal control failure, not just a vendor problem. The formalised contract (September 2024) set fees at RM215 per permit — comprising RM130 in recovery costs and RM85 in operating costs — exceeding the RM86 rate approved by the PM's own Public-Private Partnership Unit. The government had no access to the system's source code, though the contract now requires handover before expiry.
cards.d: PAC Jul 2024; The Edge Nov 2024; Bernama Mar 2025; Malaysiakini Feb 2026

cards.h: Bangladesh requested Aminul Islam's arrest through Interpol channels. Malaysia asked Bangladesh to withdraw its trafficking probes. Women domestic workers are invisible in the system.
cards.s: In October 2024, Bangladesh's NCB wrote to Malaysia's NCB requesting arrest and extradition of Aminul Islam and Ruhul Amin Swapan for trafficking, extortion, and money laundering. In May 2025, Malaysia's Human Resources Ministry asked Bangladesh to withdraw 'unsubstantiated allegations' to protect Malaysia's US TIP ranking. Meanwhile, nearly 29% of migrant domestic workers — predominantly women excluded from Employment Act protections — met ILO forced-labour indicators. Under Malaysian law, employers must pay permit fees, but enforcement is absent: workers bear the cost.
cards.d: Bloomberg Nov 2024; Bloomberg May 2025; OHCHR Nov 2025; ILO/Reuters 2023; Employment Act Section 60K

cards.h: Zahid's administration created the 10-agency restriction. Anwar's Cabinet extended the contract despite its own research centre's warning. The system persists because alternatives are limited, not just because everyone profits.
cards.s: Zahid Hamidi was Home Minister in 2015 when Bestinet's system was adopted and the 10-agency restriction was imposed on Bangladesh's 1,000+ licensed recruiters. Six Bloomberg sources say he managed the political arrangements. Anwar's Cabinet extended the contract in 2024 despite a Madani Research Centre memo warning costs had doubled. But the persistence is also structural: a 2023 open tender attracted only two bidders, one was disqualified, and the system does process visas in 14 days versus 120 before. Every coalition has left the system in place because dismantling it is costly, risky, and would disrupt labour supply.
cards.d: Bloomberg Jan 2026; C4 Center Jul 2024; The Edge Aug 2023; Home Ministry data via Hansard 2020

cards.h: This is not just a migrant worker problem or a corruption scandal — it is a government procurement failure where the product processed is human beings and the system also reaches into East Malaysia's distinct crisis
cards.text: A syndicate siphoned an estimated US$1 billion from Bangladesh. Workers arrive in debt bondage that takes 10 months' wages to repay. FWCMS does not even apply in Sabah and Sarawak, where an estimated 1 million undocumented migrants face different but equally severe exploitation under separate systems. Bestinet's response to Bloomberg's investigation was to file a RM1 billion libel suit against 10 defendants including media and politicians.

cards.h: Like outsourcing passport control to a toll booth operator who cut processing time from months to days — then discovering the operator charges five times the posted rate, has no contract, and faces an arrest request from another country
cards.text: The government handed control of its migrant worker processing to a private company, lost access to the source code, paid more than its own approved rate, and continued the arrangement after Interpol-channel arrest requests were filed. The system delivers real speed — but speed built on worker debt bondage is not efficiency. It is exploitation with a dashboard.

Digital migration systems are legitimate governance tools — and Bestinet's FWCMS genuinely reduced processing times. But speed without accountability created a monopoly that enriches connected individuals while trapping workers in debt. The fix requires open-tendering with government-owned source code, enforcing the employer-pays principle already in the Employment Act, capping recruitment fees to ILO standards, and cooperating with Bangladesh's criminal investigation instead of suppressing it. SUHAKAM and C4 have already proposed victim-centred reform frameworks. The Philippines and South Korea show government-run alternatives work. The question is not whether reform is possible — it is whether any coalition will accept the short-term disruption of dismantling a system that processes 2.3 million permits a year.
